region_of_origin: South Asia / Southeast Asia (Mangifera indica native to South and Southeast Asia)

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium fresh mango, flesh only, approximately 200 g, as commonly served as a fruit portion or dessert accompaniment in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Mango, raw — USDA FoodData Central, FDC ID 169910 (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw mango (Mangifera indica) used as primary nutrient reference. Values represent edible flesh per 100 g. HPB Singapore Foodo does not carry a standalone raw mango entry; USDA SR Legacy is the highest-priority approved source available for this raw tropical fruit.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281–2283. Mango entry: GI=51 (glucose reference).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 51 for ripe mango (Mangifera indica) sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). GL per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 51 × 29.6 / 100 ≈ 15.1, based on 200 g serving with 14.98 g carbohydrate per 100 g (available carbohydrate ≈ 14.98 − 1.6 dietary fibre = 13.38 g per 100 g; 200 g serving = 26.76 g available carbohydrate; GL = 51 × 26.76 / 100 ≈ 13.6). Revised GL = 51 × 26.76 / 100 = 13.6, rounded to 13.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (1 mg per 100 g) and moderate potassium (168 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Mango is very low in sodium and provides potassium, making it a suitable fruit choice for individuals managing hypertension; standard portion sizes (150–200 g) are appropriate within a DASH-aligned dietary pattern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 51) and high natural sugar content (13.66 g sugar per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mango has a low-to-moderate GI of 51 but is relatively high in natural sugars; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to approximately 100–150 g per serving and pair with protein or fat to attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.09 g per 100 g), zero cholesterol, and rich in vitamin C and folate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mango is very low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, and its vitamin C (36.4 mg per 100 g) and folate (43 mcg DFE per 100 g) content support vascular health; it is a cardioprotective fruit choice when consumed in moderate portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mango (Mangifera indica) is a tropical stone fruit native to South and Southeast Asia, widely cultivated and consumed across Singapore, Malaysia, India, Thailand, Vietnam, and the Philippines. The edible flesh is sweet, aromatic, and ranges in colour from pale yellow to deep orange depending on variety and ripeness. Common varieties include Alphonso (India), Carabao (Philippines), Nam Dok Mai (Thailand), and Harumanis (Malaysia/Singapore). In Singapore, fresh mango is served as a standalone fruit, incorporated into desserts such as mango pudding and mango sago, used as a topping for shaved ice (ice kachang), or served alongside seafood dishes as a condiment or garnish. Nutritionally, raw mango provides approximately 60 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates (14.98 g) as the dominant macronutrient, primarily as natural sugars (13.66 g). It is an excellent source of vitamin C (36.4 mg per 100 g, approximately 40% of daily reference intake) and vitamin A (54 mcg RAE per 100 g), and provides useful amounts of folate (43 mcg DFE), potassium (168 mg), and dietary fibre (1.6 g). Fat and protein content are negligible. The glycemic index of ripe mango is approximately 51 (low-to-moderate), making portion control relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Mango is naturally free of sodium, cholesterol, gluten, and dairy, and is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, and kosher diets. In clinical nutrition contexts, mango is considered a nutrient-dense fruit with antioxidant properties attributable to beta-carotene, vitamin C, and polyphenols.

What is the glycemic index of mango?
Can I eat mango if I have high blood pressure?
How much mango can a diabetic eat?
What is mango called in Chinese?
What is mango called in Malay?
What is mango called in Tamil?
What is mango called in Hindi?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169910), an approved primary source with exact match to raw mango. GI value of 51 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), a peer-reviewed journal. All ontology codes are defensible and verified against FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT.
notes: 1. Input record classified mango under cuisine 'Crab' and food_group 'Grains and staples' — both are incorrect metadata artefacts from the field survey seed data. Mango is a tropical fruit; food_group corrected to 'Fruits' and cuisine corrected to 'Pan-Asian / Tropical'. 2. The field survey context (served at seafood restaurants) likely refers to mango as a garnish, condiment, or dessert accompaniment rather than a standalone dish; nutritional values represent raw mango flesh per 100 g. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; naturally zero in raw fruit. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as mango is a plant food. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero as mango is a plant food with no B12. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy; raw mango does not contain vitamin D. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.14), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.07), potassium_mg (168.0), calcium_mg (11.0), iron_mg (0.16), cholesterol_mg (0.0), vitamin_c_mg (36.4), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (54.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.028), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.038), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (0.669), vitamin_b6_mg (0.119), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.0), folate_mcg_dfe (43.0), vitamin_d_mcg (0.0), vitamin_e_mg (0.9), phosphorus_mg (14.0), magnesium_mg (10.0), zinc_mg (0.09), selenium_mcg (0.6), water_g (83.46) — all sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 169910. 8. GL per serving recalculated: available carbohydrate per 200 g serving = (14.98 − 1.6) × 2 = 26.76 g; GL = 51 × 26.76 / 100 = 13.6. gi_source notes field corrected accordingly. 9. No -1 sentinel values remain in per_100g; all nutrients are reported by USDA SR Legacy for raw mango.
